turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kagan has the opinion of the Court in case 21-984 Helix v. Hewitt.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The Fair Labor Standards Act let's call it the FLSA guarantees that covered workers receive overtime pay, time and a half pay when they work more than 40 hours a week. But an employee isn't covered and so isn't entitled to overtime pay if he's what the statute calls a bona fide executive. Agency regulations from the Department of Labor spell out what that term means. Under those rules, an employee is a bona fide executive if #1, he's paid on what's called a salary basis. #2, that salary exceeds a certain level and #3, he has various kinds of managerial responsibilities. This case is about the first part of that test. It's about what it means to be paid on a salary basis to get a salary as opposed to wages. More specifically the question is this. Whether a high earning employee is paid on a salary basis when his paycheck is based solely on a daily rate so that he receives a certain amount if he works one day, twice as much for two days, three times as much for three and so on. The worker is Michael Hewitt. Mr. Hewitt worked for a company called Helix Energy Group on an offshore oil rig. He was paid well for his work over $200,000.00 a year. When he was on duty, he worked hard usually 84 hours a week and he didn't get any overtime for the hours over 40. Hewitt thought he should get overtime pay so he brought this suit. Helix disagreed. It said that Hewitt was a bona fide executive so he exempt from the FLSA and wasn't entitled to overtime. Now everyone agreed that Hewitt had managerial responsibilities and everyone agreed that if he was paid a salary, it was above the level needed for bona fide executives. What they disagreed about was whether Hewitt in fact received a salary. Whether he was paid on a salary basis; if he was, he was exempt from the FLSA as a bona fide executive. If he wasn't, he was covered and should have been paid overtime. Now, thinking about that question, the most important thing you have to know is this. I've saved the most important fact for last. Hewitt was paid by the day. So if he was sick or otherwise indisposed for a few days, his pay would be docked. His pay otherwise put was variable. It amounted to his daily rate times the number of days he worked in any given pay period. The more days he worked, the more he got paid. The fewer days he worked, the less he got paid. Today, we hold that because of that fact, Hewitt was not paid on a salary basis. We reach that holding by looking carefully at the language and structure of the regulations. We also note that what the regulations say matches up pretty well with what ordinary people mean when they say someone gets a salary. They mean that he gets a steady and predictable paycheck, which doesn't vary based on the number of days or hours he works and that's just how the regulations explain what it means to be paid a salary. They say and I'm going to be doing quoting here, ?an employee gets a salary again as opposed to wages when he gets a predetermined amount that can't be changed because of the number of days or hours he actually works?. They say he has to receive his full salary for any week in which he works at all and they say to put it most clearly that he must be paid on a weekly or monthly basis not on a daily or hourly one. Case closed. Hewitt was paid on a daily basis. His paycheck did vary with the number of days and hours he worked so he wasn't paid a salary. Actually, this case is even stronger than that because there's a separate part of the regulations that says that in certain narrow conditions, a day rate worker is paid a salary but everyone agrees including Helix that those conditions aren't met in this case. The relevance of that separate part of the regulations is only this. It shows that if those conditions aren't met, a day rate worker is not paid a salary. So Hewitt wasn't paid a salary. He is entitled to overtime and that's so even though his daily rate was high and he took home a fair bit of money. This Court has long said that workers may not be deprived of the FLSA's overtime benefits just because they are well paid and one problem with Helix's view is that it would deprive many workers who make far less money than Hewitt of overtime pay. Justice Gorsuch has filed a dissenting opinion. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Alito joins.
